title: What to Pack for a Boat Charter Day: The Practical Checklist
topic: What to bring on a boat charter day
html:
A day on the water can be one of the best parts of any trip, but the difference between a brilliant outing and an uncomfortable one often comes down to what you remember to pack. Boats have limited storage and no shops on board, so a little planning goes a long way. Whether you are about to rent a boat for a family afternoon or a longer coastal cruise, this practical checklist will help you arrive prepared and ready to enjoy the day.

Sun protection comes first

The sun feels far stronger at sea than on land, because water and pale decks reflect light straight back at you and the breeze hides the heat until it is too late. Treat sun protection as the single most important thing you bring. Pack the following:

  • High-factor sunscreen, reef-safe if you plan to swim, and reapply it more often than you think you need to.
  • A wide-brimmed or secure hat, ideally one with a chin cord so it does not fly off in the wind.
  • Sunglasses with a strap, polarised lenses if possible, which cut glare and let you see into the water.
  • A light long-sleeved layer for the hottest part of the afternoon, when shade on a boat can be scarce.

Clothing and footwear that works at sea

Dress in layers. Mornings and the run back to port can be surprisingly cool once the boat picks up speed, even on a hot day. Bring a light windproof jacket or jumper alongside your swimwear and a spare set of dry clothes in case you get splashed. Quick-drying fabrics are far more comfortable than cotton, which stays damp for hours.

Footwear matters more than people expect. Many owners ask guests to remove hard-soled shoes to protect the deck, so pack soft-soled deck shoes or simply plan to go barefoot. Flip-flops are handy for the pontoon but easy to lose overboard, so keep them in your bag once you are aboard. If you are still deciding which kind of vessel suits your group, it is worth comparing options before you commit to a boat rental, since deck space and shade vary enormously between boats.

Food, drink and staying hydrated

Check with the operator first, as some charters include refreshments, a cooler or even a crew who prepare lunch, while others expect you to bring your own. When in doubt, bring more water than you think you will drink. Sun, salt and wind are dehydrating, and there is no tap to refill from once you leave the marina.

  • Plenty of water, ideally in reusable bottles you can refill from a larger container.
  • Easy snacks such as fruit, nuts, sandwiches or anything that does not melt or crush in a bag.
  • A small cool bag with ice packs if drinks are not provided.
  • A bin bag so you can carry your rubbish back to shore and leave the boat as you found it.
If anyone in your group is prone to seasickness, take a remedy before you leave the dock rather than after symptoms start. Ginger sweets, acupressure bands and over-the-counter tablets all help, and a gentle first hour at anchor lets people find their sea legs.

Documents, money and the small essentials

Keep your paperwork dry and within reach. Most charters need some form of identification, and if you intend to take the helm yourself you may be asked to show a boating licence or proof of experience. Confirm exactly what is required when you hire a boat, because rules differ from one country and one vessel to the next. Bring a little cash too, as small marinas, fuel docks and beach bars do not always take cards.

A few small items make a big difference over a long day:

  • A dry bag or sealed pouch for phones, keys, wallets and cameras.
  • A power bank, since you will use your phone for photos and navigation.
  • A travel towel and a swimming costume worn under your clothes to save time.
  • Basic medication, plasters and any personal prescriptions.

Extras that make the day better

Once the essentials are covered, a few thoughtful additions turn a good charter into a memorable one. Snorkelling gear lets you make the most of clear water, and a waterproof speaker, a deck of cards or a paddleboard can keep everyone happy at anchor. A simple first-aid kit and a whistle are sensible even on a calm day. Before you head out, take a moment to browse boats and read the listing notes, as many owners detail what is already on board so you do not double up on bulky kit.

A final word before you cast off

The golden rule is to pack light but pack smart: sun protection, water, layers, dry storage and your documents are the non-negotiables, and everything else is a bonus. Arriving prepared means you can relax the moment you step aboard and let the crew or the open water take over. When you are ready to plan your next outing, you can compare hundreds of vessels worldwide and arrange your trip in minutes at boathire24.com, the marketplace that connects travellers and boat owners across the globe.
